questions.stem: A 58-year-old man has 8 months of heartburn and sour regurgitation, worse lying down, with recent weight gain (BMI 32). No dysphagia, weight loss, bleeding, or anemia. Most appropriate next step?
options: Clinical diagnosis of GERD with lifestyle measures and a PPI
questions.explain: Typical heartburn/regurgitation without alarm features is a clinical diagnosis. Manage with weight loss, head-of-bed elevation, avoiding late meals, and a PPI. Endoscopy is reserved for alarm features (dysphagia, weight loss, bleeding, anemia, persistent vomiting).

questions.stem: A 25-year-old develops 12 hours of forceful vomiting from food poisoning. After repeated retching, he vomits a small amount of bright red blood. He is stable, with no liver disease or NSAID use. Most likely diagnosis?
questions.explain: Hematemesis following repeated forceful vomiting/retching in an otherwise healthy person is the classic picture of a Mallory-Weiss tear (mucosal laceration at the gastroesophageal junction).

questions.stem: A 61-year-old with long-standing T2DM and neuropathy has 6 months of nausea, vomiting of undigested food, and early satiety. Endoscopy is normal; gastric emptying study shows delayed emptying. QTc is normal. Best chronic prokinetic?
questions.explain: Diabetic gastroparesis. Domperidone (peripheral D2 antagonist) is favored chronically because it doesn't readily cross the blood-brain barrier (fewer CNS effects than metoclopramide), though QTc should be monitored.

questions.stem: A 30-year-old develops bloody diarrhea, fever, and severe cramps after eating undercooked chicken, with 10 stools/day and lightheadedness. A friend offers loperamide. Best advice?
options: Take loperamide — it shortens invasive diarrhea
Take diphenoxylate/atropine instead, which is safer
Avoid anti-motility agents; focus on hydration and appropriate stool testing
questions.explain: Bloody diarrhea with fever suggests invasive/inflammatory colitis (e.g., Campylobacter). Avoid anti-motility agents (loperamide, diphenoxylate) — they risk toxic megacolon/prolonged infection. Prioritize hydration and targeted stool testing.

questions.stem: A 42-year-old woman with autoimmune hypothyroidism has fatigue, bloating, and chronic non-bloody diarrhea. Hb 102, MCV 72, ferritin 2. She is not vegetarian and has no overt bleeding. Most likely diagnosis?
questions.explain: Celiac disease injures the duodenum, where iron is preferentially absorbed → iron-deficiency anemia (microcytic, low ferritin). It associates with other autoimmune disease (e.g., thyroid). Confirm with tissue transglutaminase IgA + total IgA while still eating gluten, then duodenal biopsy.

questions.stem: A 28-year-old woman with Crohn's has had 180 cm of distal ileum resected over two operations; disease is now controlled on a biologic. She reports greasy, floating, foul-smelling stools and weight loss. Mechanism?
questions.explain: The terminal ileum reabsorbs bile acids. Large ileal resection depletes the bile-acid pool → impaired micelle formation → fat malabsorption and steatorrhea (greasy, floating, foul-smelling stools).

questions.stem: A man with newly diagnosed moderate-severe Crohn's improves dramatically on prednisone and asks to stay on it long-term to prevent relapse. Best response?
options: Yes — prednisone is ideal long-term maintenance
No — prednisone is for induction, not maintenance, due to serious long-term toxicity
Yes, but only combined with loperamide
Prednisone is only used in ulcerative colitis
questions.explain: Corticosteroids are for induction of remission, not maintenance: chronic use causes osteoporosis, adrenal suppression, hyperglycemia, infection risk, and avascular necrosis. Maintenance uses immunomodulators/biologics.

questions.stem: A 65-year-old woman has severe RUQ pain, jaundice, fever, confusion, HR 125, BP 80/40. Ultrasound shows gallstones with dilated intra- and extra-hepatic ducts. Diagnosis and definitive treatment?
options: Acute cholecystitis; oral antibiotics and discharge
Ascending cholangitis; resuscitation, broad-spectrum antibiotics, and urgent ERCP decompression
questions.explain: Fever + RUQ pain + jaundice (Charcot triad) plus hypotension and altered mentation (Reynolds pentad) = ascending cholangitis. Treat with resuscitation, broad antibiotics, and urgent biliary decompression by ERCP.

questions.stem: A 70-year-old with a prior laparotomy has 24 hours of crampy pain, bilious vomiting, distension, and obstipation. X-ray shows dilated small-bowel loops. No fever, no peritonitis, normal lactate; CT shows a transition point without ischemia. Most likely cause and initial management?
options: Adhesions; NPO, IV fluids, electrolyte correction, NG decompression, close surgical observation
Colon cancer; immediate hemicolectomy without staging
Ogilvie syndrome; neostigmine before excluding mechanical obstruction
questions.explain: Adhesions are the most common cause of SBO after prior abdominal surgery. A stable, non-ischemic, partial/simple SBO is initially managed conservatively: bowel rest, IV fluids, electrolyte correction, NG decompression, and serial reassessment for signs needing surgery.

questions.stem: A 44-year-old with imaging-confirmed acute appendicitis is awaiting the OR. Six hours later she becomes confused: HR 140, BP 80/45, O₂ 86%, lactate 9. Best next step?
options: Discharge with oral antibiotics and return precautions
Immediate colonoscopy to exclude colon cancer
Aggressive resuscitation, broad-spectrum IV antibiotics, urgent source control, ICU/surgical escalation
Delay all intervention until CT confirms perforation
questions.explain: She has septic shock with organ dysfunction (hypotension, hypoxemia, lactate 9) from an intra-abdominal source. Prioritize resuscitation, early broad antibiotics, and urgent source control — do not delay for perfect imaging in refractory shock.

questions.stem: A 43-year-old man with iron-deficiency anemia undergoes colonoscopy revealing an ascending-colon mass; biopsy shows adenocarcinoma. CT shows no metastases and CEA is drawn. Most appropriate initial definitive treatment?
options: Rubber-band ligation, since bleeding hemorrhoids explain the anemia
Oncologic right hemicolectomy with lymphadenectomy; adjuvant therapy by final stage
questions.explain: Biopsy-proven non-metastatic right-colon adenocarcinoma is treated with oncologic right hemicolectomy and lymphadenectomy; final pathology dictates adjuvant chemotherapy. Hemorrhoids are a diagnosis of exclusion and never explain iron-deficiency anemia until cancer is excluded.

questions.stem: A 48-year-old man with heavy alcohol use has jaundice, ascites, and asterixis. ALT 120, AST 290, bilirubin 450 µmol/L, albumin 24, INR 2.9. Paracentesis: SAAG 14, neutrophils 150/mm³. Maddrey discriminant function 65. Most appropriate specific therapy?
options: Ceftriaxone, to treat spontaneous bacterial peritonitis
Prednisolone, to suppress cytokine-mediated hepatic inflammation
questions.explain: Severe alcoholic hepatitis (AST:ALT > 2:1, Maddrey ≥ 32) is treated with prednisolone to suppress cytokine-mediated injury. The ascitic neutrophil count is < 250, so there's no SBP contraindicating steroids.

questions.stem: A 60-year-old with HCV cirrhosis presents with hematemesis, tachycardia, and hypotension. After airway protection and resuscitation, you prepare for urgent endoscopy. Which three drugs should be given before endoscopy?
questions.explain: For suspected variceal bleeding: octreotide (splanchnic vasoconstriction), ceftriaxone (prophylaxis against SBP/infection improves outcomes), and a PPI/pantoprazole (covers a possible bleeding ulcer) — then urgent band ligation.

questions.stem: A 60-year-old man's diagnostic paracentesis reports an ascitic albumin of 34 g/L; his serum albumin is 36 g/L. What is the SAAG and most likely etiology?
options: 2 g/L; an exudative process such as malignancy or infection
questions.explain: SAAG = serum − ascitic albumin = 36 − 34 = 2 g/L. A SAAG < 11 g/L indicates an exudative process (peritoneal malignancy or infection), not portal hypertension (which gives SAAG ≥ 11).

questions.stem: A 72-year-old woman has 2 months of new epigastric pain, early satiety, and 6 kg unintentional weight loss. CBC shows microcytic anemia; she denies NSAIDs; her brother died of gastric cancer. Which feature most changes management away from empiric therapy?
options: Family history, weight loss, and iron-deficiency anemia
questions.explain: Alarm features — weight loss, iron-deficiency anemia, age ≥60 with new symptoms, and family history of upper-GI malignancy — warrant prompt endoscopy rather than empiric dyspepsia treatment.

questions.stem: A 52-year-old woman reports difficulty swallowing both solids and liquids from the very onset of her symptoms, with occasional regurgitation of undigested food. Which category of disorder does this pattern suggest?
options: A motility disorder such as achalasia
questions.explain: Dysphagia to solids AND liquids from the start points to an esophageal motility disorder (achalasia). Trouble with solids first that progresses to liquids suggests a mechanical/structural cause (stricture or, if progressive with weight loss, cancer).

questions.stem: A patient with prior peptic ulcer disease is on a daily PPI and you want to confirm H. pylori eradication. What must you do for an accurate urea breath test or stool antigen test?
options: No preparation is needed; PPIs do not affect results
Stop the PPI before testing, as it can cause false negatives
Use serology instead, which confirms eradication
Test only during active GI bleeding
questions.explain: PPIs cause false-negative active-infection tests (urea breath/stool antigen), so they should be held before testing when safe. Serology reflects exposure, not active infection or cure.

questions.stem: An 82-year-old nursing-home resident with dementia and chronic constipation (on iron tablets) develops 'diarrhea' and fecal incontinence, poor appetite, and occasional vomiting. Most likely diagnosis?
questions.explain: In an older adult with chronic constipation, new 'diarrhea'/incontinence is classically overflow diarrhea from fecal impaction. Iron worsens constipation. Always exclude impaction before labeling true diarrhea.

questions.stem: A 48-year-old woman has RUQ pain for 10 hours with fever, nausea, and a positive Murphy sign. WBC is elevated; ultrasound shows gallbladder wall thickening, pericholecystic fluid, and stones. Best management?
options: NPO, IV fluids, analgesia, antibiotics, and early laparoscopic cholecystectomy
questions.explain: Pain > 6 h, fever, positive Murphy, leukocytosis, and ultrasound findings = acute cholecystitis. Manage with NPO, IV fluids, analgesia, antibiotics, and early laparoscopic cholecystectomy. ERCP is for CBD stones/cholangitis, not gallbladder-only stones.

questions.stem: A 60-year-old man has LLQ pain, fever, and localized peritonitis. CT shows sigmoid diverticulitis with fat stranding but no abscess or free air. Best confirmatory test and management principle?
options: Colonoscopy now with biopsy of the inflamed segment
CT abdomen/pelvis; supportive care with selective antibiotics, then colon evaluation after recovery
Immediate elective sigmoid colectomy for this first episode
questions.explain: CT is the best single test for acute diverticulitis. Manage supportively (selective antibiotics by severity). Avoid colonoscopy acutely (perforation risk), but arrange follow-up colon evaluation after recovery because cancer can mimic diverticulitis.

questions.stem: A 54-year-old woman has months of fatigue and intense pruritus, with a history of Sjögren syndrome. Labs: ALT 45, AST 40, ALP 510, GGT 400, bilirubin 18; ultrasound shows no biliary dilation. Which test confirms the diagnosis, and what is first-line treatment?
questions.explain: A cholestatic pattern (high ALP/GGT, near-normal ALT/AST) with fatigue, pruritus, and an autoimmune association is primary biliary cholangitis. AMA is diagnostic; ursodeoxycholic acid is first-line.

questions.stem: A 56-year-old man with alcoholic cirrhosis is brought in for personality change, napping by day and pacing at night, and disorientation to time (but not person/place). On wrist extension he shows a brief, non-rhythmic flexion. West Haven stage?
questions.explain: Disorientation to time, behavioral/personality change, sleep-wake inversion, and — critically — asterixis mark West Haven Stage 2 hepatic encephalopathy. Asterixis first appears at Stage 2.

cards.prompt: Difficulty swallowing both solids and liquids from the onset, with regurgitation of undigested food. Category?
cards.explain: Solids and liquids from the start = motility (achalasia). Solids-first then liquids = mechanical/structural.

cards.text: Before a urea breath test or stool antigen for H. pylori, stop the {{PPI}} for about {{2 weeks}}, otherwise you get {{false negatives}}.
cards.explain: PPIs suppress the organism enough to make active-infection tests falsely negative.

cards.prompt: Match antiemetic to its receptor target:
cards.explain: Receptor-target matching is the basis for choosing antiemetics by cause.

cards.prompt: Bloody diarrhea with fever after undercooked chicken, 10 stools/day. Which is contraindicated?
cards.explain: Anti-motility agents in invasive/inflammatory (bloody, febrile) diarrhea risk toxic megacolon / prolonged infection — avoid loperamide.

cards.prompt: Assign each feature to Crohn or UC:
cards.explain: Crohn = patchy, transmural, complicated; UC = continuous, mucosal, curable by colectomy.

cards.text: The terminal ileum absorbs {{B12}} and {{bile acids}}, so ileal Crohn or resection causes B12 deficiency and {{bile-acid}} (or fat) malabsorption.
cards.explain: Large ileal resection depletes the bile-acid pool → steatorrhea; cholestyramine helps bile-acid diarrhea.

cards.prompt: Order the management of a variceal bleed:
cards.explain: Ceftriaxone improves survival in cirrhotic GI bleeds; the β-blocker is prophylaxis, not for the acute bleed.

cards.prompt: RUQ pain, jaundice, fever, PLUS hypotension and confusion, with dilated ducts and stones. Diagnosis & definitive step?
cards.explain: Charcot triad + hypotension/AMS = Reynolds pentad = ascending cholangitis → resuscitate, antibiotics, urgent ERCP decompression.

cards.prompt: An elderly patient with atrial fibrillation has severe abdominal pain but a soft, near-normal exam. What must you suspect, and why the mismatch?
cards.answer: Acute mesenteric ischemia (embolic, from AF). Early on the visceral ischemia causes severe pain while peritoneal signs are minimal — "pain out of proportion to exam" — until infarction/peritonitis develops. Get CT angiography and lactate; it's a surgical/revascularization emergency.
cards.explain: AF + pain-out-of-proportion is the classic embolic mesenteric ischemia stem.

cards.text: Acute pancreatitis = any 2 of 3: epigastric pain to the back, {{lipase}} above 3× ULN, and imaging. The two commonest causes are {{gallstones}} and {{alcohol}}.
cards.explain: Management is aggressive fluids, analgesia, early enteral feeding; antibiotics only for infected necrosis.

cards.prompt: Serum albumin 36, ascitic albumin 22 g/L. SAAG and most likely mechanism?
cards.explain: SAAG = 36 − 22 = 14 (≥11) → portal hypertension (transudate). A SAAG < 11 points to malignancy or infection.

cards.prompt: Match each Hep B result to its meaning:
cards.explain: In the window period only anti-HBc IgM is positive; isolated anti-HBs means vaccination.

cards.text: Severe alcoholic hepatitis shows an AST:ALT ratio above {{2}}:1; a Maddrey discriminant function ≥ {{32}} is treated with {{prednisolone}} (unless active infection or GI bleeding).
cards.explain: AST rarely exceeds a few hundred in alcohol — transaminases in the thousands mean ischemia, virus, or toxin.

guides.title: Topic 07 Upper GI
guides.html:
◇ Topic Reference · 07 ◇

Upper GI

GI I · ~2 pp
trigger → answer
Physiology & the master switch: alarm features
Acid: parietal cells secrete H⁺ via the H⁺/K⁺-ATPase (PPI target), driven by gastrin, histamine (H2), ACh. Mucosal defense = mucus/bicarbonate + prostaglandins (why NSAIDs ulcerate).
Alarm features ("VBAD-W") that mandate endoscopy over empiric therapy: Vomiting (persistent), Bleeding/anemia, Age ≥60 with new symptoms, Dysphagia/odynophagia, Weight loss.
Dysphagia logic: solids→then liquids = mechanical; solids AND liquids from onset = motility. Transfer difficulty/cough on swallow = oropharyngeal.
Antiemetic targets: 5-HT3 (chemo/GI), D2 (CTZ), H1/M1 (vestibular), NK1 (chemo).
★ Upper-GI emergencies
Perforated peptic ulcer — sudden severe epigastric pain, rigid "board-like" abdomen, free air under diaphragm. Resuscitate, IV PPI + antibiotics, urgent surgery. (Anterior duodenal ulcers perforate; posterior ones bleed from the gastroduodenal artery.)
Boerhaave (esophageal rupture) — forceful vomiting → transmural tear: severe chest/epigastric pain, subcutaneous emphysema, ± left pleural effusion (Mackler triad). CT/contrast study; surgical/emergency management. (Contrast with the mucosal-only Mallory-Weiss tear, which usually self-limits.)

GERD

Typical heartburn + acid regurgitation, worse supine/postprandial
Diagnose clinical if no alarm features → trial PPI + lifestyle (weight loss, head-of-bed elevation, avoid late/large meals & triggers)
Endoscopy if alarm features or refractory
Complications erosive esophagitis, peptic stricture, Barrett esophagus (intestinal metaplasia → adenocarcinoma → surveillance)
Refractory pH-impedance + manometry; consider fundoplication

Dyspepsia & H. pylori

<60, no alarm test-and-treat H. pylori (urea breath / stool antigen) or empiric PPI
≥60 or alarm upper endoscopy
Functional dyspepsia symptoms with normal endoscopy
H. pylori testing rules
Stop PPI ~2 wk before urea breath/stool antigen (false negatives)
Treat quadruple / clarithromycin-based triple therapy; confirm eradication after

Peptic ulcer disease

Duodenal commoner, H. pylori ~90%, pain relieved by food, younger
Gastric NSAIDs, pain worse with food, weight loss — biopsy to exclude malignancy
Zollinger-Ellison gastrinoma — multiple/refractory/distal ulcers, diarrhea, ↑gastrin; MEN1
Complications bleeding (posterior duodenal → GDA), perforation (anterior → free air), gastric outlet obstruction
Treat PPI, eradicate H. pylori, stop NSAIDs

Dysphagia — sorting it out

Oropharyngeal trouble initiating, cough/choke/aspiration — neuromuscular (stroke, Parkinson, myasthenia, ALS)
Esophageal — mechanical (solids first)
Peptic stricture GERD history, progressive
Schatzki ring intermittent solid food (steakhouse) dysphagia
Cancer progressive + weight loss, age/smoking/alcohol (SCC) or Barrett (adeno)
Eosinophilic esophagitis young, atopic, food impaction; rings on EGD
Esophageal — motility (solids + liquids)
Achalasia LES won't relax + aperistalsis; bird-beak on barium, manometry; treat pneumatic dilation/myotomy/botox. Exclude pseudoachalasia (GEJ tumor)
Others diffuse esophageal spasm, scleroderma

Nausea/vomiting & gastroparesis

5-HT3 antagonist ondansetron — chemo/post-op/GI (QT)
D2 antagonist metoclopramide (also prokinetic; tardive dyskinesia), prochlorperazine, haloperidol; domperidone (less CNS)
H1/M1 dimenhydrinate, scopolamine — vestibular/motion
NK1 aprepitant — chemo
Gastroparesis diabetic; vomiting undigested food, early satiety; gastric emptying study; prokinetics (domperidone/metoclopramide/erythromycin) + dietary changes

Pitfalls

Gastric ulcer always biopsy — exclude malignancy
Stop PPI before H. pylori breath/stool testing
New dyspepsia ≥60 scope, don't just treat
Solids+liquids from onset motility (achalasia) — but exclude GEJ cancer
Don't forget Barrett surveillance after chronic GERD
Topic 07 / Upper GI (detailed) · generated from your 11-week guides · verify against course & clinical guidelines · review aid only

guides.title: Topic 08 Lower GI
guides.html:
◇ Topic Reference · 08 ◇

Lower GI — Diarrhea · Malabsorption · IBD

GI II · ~2 pp
trigger → answer
Where things are absorbed (predicts the deficiency)
Duodenum: iron, calcium → celiac → iron-deficiency anemia.
Jejunum: most nutrients, folate.
Terminal ileum: B12 + bile acids → Crohn/resection → B12 deficiency + bile-acid (or fat) malabsorption.
Colon: water/electrolytes. Osmotic diarrhea stops with fasting (↑stool osmotic gap); secretory persists fasting (large volume); inflammatory = blood/pus.
★ Lower-GI emergencies
Toxic megacolon — colonic dilation >6 cm + systemic toxicity (fever, tachycardia, ↑WBC), in severe UC or C. difficile. Avoid anti-motility agents/opioids. IV fluids, steroids (if IBD)/antibiotics, decompression; surgery (colectomy) if no improvement or perforation.
EHEC O157:H7 → HUS — bloody diarrhea + microangiopathic hemolytic anemia, thrombocytopenia, AKI. Do NOT give antibiotics or anti-motility agents (↑ Shiga toxin release / HUS risk); supportive care.

Diarrhea — the approach

Acute (<2 wk) usually infectious; rehydrate
Watery/non-inflammatory norovirus, ETEC (traveler's), viral, food toxins — supportive ± loperamide OK
Bloody/inflammatory Shigella, Salmonella, Campylobacter, EHEC, C. diff, Entamoeba — avoid loperamide, stool studies
Chronic (>4 wk) osmotic (fasting-responsive), secretory (large-volume, persists fasting), inflammatory (calprotectin↑, scope), fatty/steatorrhea
Overflow elderly + constipation/impaction presenting as "diarrhea"

Malabsorption

Celiac gluten → duodenal villous atrophy; iron-def anemia, dermatitis herpetiformis; tTG-IgA + total IgA on gluten → biopsy; assoc T1DM/thyroid/IgA def; ↑lymphoma; lifelong GFD
Lactose intolerance osmotic diarrhea/bloating after dairy
SIBO bloating; low B12, high folate; breath test; antibiotics
Bile-acid malabsorption ileal disease/resection → watery diarrhea → cholestyramine
Pancreatic insufficiency chronic pancreatitis/CF → steatorrhea, ↓fecal elastase → enzyme replacement

IBD — Crohn vs UC

CrohnUC
Distributionmouth→anus, skip lesions, TI/rightcontinuous from rectum
Depthtransmural (fistula/stricture/abscess)mucosal
Stool± blood, malabsorptionbloody + tenesmus
Smokingworsensprotective
Cureno (surgery for complications)colectomy curative
Othergranulomas, cobblestone, gallstones/oxalate stones, B12toxic megacolon, ↑CRC, PSC
Extraintestinal arthritis, erythema nodosum, pyoderma gangrenosum, uveitis/episcleritis, PSC, ankylosing spondylitis

IBD — work-up & treatment

Work-up fecal calprotectin, colonoscopy + biopsy, cross-sectional imaging (Crohn)
5-ASA mild UC induction/maintenance
Corticosteroids induction ONLY — not maintenance (osteoporosis, adrenal suppression, infection, AVN)
Immunomodulators azathioprine/6-MP, methotrexate (maintenance)
Biologics anti-TNF (infliximab/adalimumab), vedolizumab, ustekinumab — screen TB/HepB first
Surgery UC → curative colectomy; Crohn → for strictures/fistulae/abscess (recurs)

IBS · C. difficile · pitfalls

IBS (Rome IV)
Criteria recurrent abdominal pain ≥1 day/wk ×3 mo + ≥2 of (defecation-related, change in frequency, change in form); no alarm features
Treat low-FODMAP, fiber, antispasmodics; loperamide (IBS-D), laxatives/linaclotide (IBS-C), TCA/SSRI
C. difficile
Risk recent antibiotics (clindamycin, fluoroquinolones, cephalosporins), healthcare exposure
Dx/Tx stool toxin/PCR; oral vancomycin or fidaxomicin, stop offending antibiotic; recurrent → fecal transplant
Pitfalls
No antibiotics in EHEC (HUS risk); no loperamide in bloody diarrhea
Calprotectin separates inflammatory (IBD) from functional (IBS)
Steroids ≠ maintenance in IBD
Topic 08 / Lower GI (detailed) · generated from your 11-week guides · verify against course & clinical guidelines · review aid only

guides.title: Topic 09 Liver and Biliary
guides.html:
◇ Topic Reference · 09 ◇

Liver & Biliary

GI III + GI IV · ~2–3 pp
trigger → answer
Reading LFTs & jaundice
Hepatocellular = ↑↑ALT/AST; cholestatic = ↑↑ALP/GGT. Isolated ↑ALP → check GGT to confirm it's liver (vs bone).
Synthetic function: ↑INR is the earliest marker of acute failure; ↓albumin reflects chronicity. AST:ALT >2:1 → alcohol; values in the thousands → ischemia/toxin/virus.
Unconjugated (indirect) hyperbilirubinemia: hemolysis, Gilbert, ineffective erythropoiesis — no bilirubin in urine.
Conjugated (direct): hepatocellular or obstructive — dark urine; pale stools + dark urine = obstruction.
★ Hepatobiliary emergencies
Variceal hemorrhage — resuscitate + airway → octreotide + ceftriaxone + PPI → urgent band ligation; balloon tamponade/TIPS as rescue. (β-blocker is prophylaxis, not for the acute bleed.)
Ascending cholangitis — Charcot (fever/RUQ/jaundice) ± Reynolds (hypotension/AMS) → resuscitate + broad antibiotics + urgent ERCP decompression.
Acute liver failure — encephalopathy + coagulopathy (no prior cirrhosis); acetaminophen #1 → NAC; King's College criteria for transplant.

Viral hepatitis

A fecal-oral; acute only, no chronic; HAV IgM = acute
E fecal-oral; fulminant in pregnancy
B blood/sexual/vertical; can become chronic → cirrhosis/HCC
C blood; often chronic; Ab → confirm RNA; DAAs cure
D needs HBV co/superinfection
Hep B serology
HBsAg active infection
anti-HBs immunity (recovery or vaccine)
anti-HBc IgM acute (only marker in window period)
HBeAg high replication/infectivity
anti-HBs alone vaccinated

Cirrhosis & complications

Portal HTN varices, splenomegaly, ascites, caput medusae
Ascites — SAAG ≥11 = portal HTN (transudate); <11 = malignancy/infection (exudate). Treat: Na restriction + spironolactone + furosemide; LVP + albumin; refractory → TIPS
SBP ascitic PMN >250 → cefotaxime + albumin (prevent HRS); prophylaxis norfloxacin
Hepatic encephalopathy ammonia; precipitants (GI bleed, infection, constipation, electrolytes, sedatives); asterixis = stage 2; lactulose + rifaximin
Hepatorenal syndrome functional AKI; albumin + vasoconstrictor (terlipressin); transplant
HCC surveillance US ± AFP q6mo in cirrhosis

Specific liver diseases

Alcoholic hepatitis AST:ALT >2:1; Maddrey ≥32 → prednisolone (not if active infection/GIB)
NAFLD/NASH metabolic syndrome; commonest chronic liver disease → weight loss
Autoimmune hepatitis young ♀, ↑IgG, ANA/ASMA (I) or anti-LKM1 (II) → steroids + azathioprine
PBC middle-aged ♀, pruritus, cholestatic, AMA, ↑IgM → UDCA
PSC ♂ + IBD/UC, beaded ducts on MRCP, p-ANCA; ↑cholangiocarcinoma
Hemochromatosis HFE C282Y; ↑ferritin/transferrin sat; bronze diabetes, cardiomyopathy, arthropathy → phlebotomy
Wilson ATP7B; ↓ceruloplasmin, KF rings, neuropsych, hemolysis, young → chelation/zinc
A1AT deficiency liver disease + panacinar emphysema

Gallstone disease — the spectrum

Risk cholesterol stones: female, fat, forty, fertile; pigment: hemolysis
Biliary colic postprandial RUQ pain <6 h, no fever, normal labs → elective lap chole
Acute cholecystitis >6 h, fever, Murphy+, leukocytosis, US wall thickening → abx + early lap chole
Choledocholithiasis jaundice, dilated CBD, ↑bilirubin/ALP → ERCP then chole
Ascending cholangitis Charcot/Reynolds → urgent ERCP (see emergencies)
Gallstone pancreatitis ↑lipase; supportive; cholecystectomy same admission
Courvoisier painless jaundice + palpable non-tender gallbladder → malignancy (pancreatic/cholangio)

Pitfalls

Window period only anti-HBc IgM positive (HBsAg gone, anti-HBs not yet up)
SAAG direction ≥11 portal HTN; <11 exudate — easy to invert
β-blocker prophylaxis only — use octreotide for the acute variceal bleed
Acetaminophen even "therapeutic" doses can injure malnourished/alcoholic livers
Transplant active alcohol/substance use = absolute contraindication
Confirm SBP paracentesis before committing to long antibiotic course
Topic 09 / Liver & Biliary (detailed) · generated from your 11-week guides · verify against course & clinical guidelines · review aid only

guides.title: Topic 10 Acute Abdomen Surgery
guides.html:
◇ Topic Reference · 10 ◇

Acute Abdomen & GI Surgery

GI III · ~2 pp
trigger → answer
Reading abdominal pain
Referred pain by embryonic gut: foregut → epigastric, midgut → periumbilical, hindgut → suprapubic. Pain that migrates & localizes (visceral → parietal) signals peritoneal irritation (e.g., appendicitis).
Peritonism = rigidity, rebound, guarding, pain on movement/cough → surgical abdomen. Pain out of proportion to a soft exam → mesenteric ischemia.
Always: resuscitate, NPO, analgesia (doesn't mask the exam), labs + lactate, β-hCG in women, erect CXR/CT.
"Surgery now" triggers: free air/perforation, generalized peritonitis, ischemia, closed-loop obstruction, uncontrolled bleeding, septic source needing control.
★ The acute-abdomen emergencies
Perforation / peritonitis — rigid abdomen + free air → resuscitate, broad antibiotics, urgent laparotomy.
Intra-abdominal sepsis/septic shock — resuscitate + early antibiotics + source control (drain/operate); don't delay for perfect imaging in refractory shock.
Acute mesenteric ischemia — pain out of proportion, lactate↑; CT angiography; emergent revascularization/resection. Suspect with AF (embolic).

Appendicitis

Classic periumbilical pain → migrates to RLQ (McBurney), anorexia, nausea, low fever
Signs rebound, Rovsing, psoas, obturator
Dx clinical + CT/US (US in young/pregnant); appendicolith
Treat laparoscopic appendectomy + antibiotics; complicated (abscess) → antibiotics ± drainage, interval appendectomy

Acute pancreatitis

Causes gallstones #1, alcohol #2, hypertriglyceridemia, hypercalcemia, ERCP, drugs (GET SMASHED)
Diagnose (2 of 3) epigastric pain → back · lipase >3× ULN · imaging
Severity organ failure, CRP, BISAP/APACHE; CT for necrosis
Manage aggressive IV fluids, analgesia, early enteral feeding; treat cause (ERCP if cholangitis/obstruction; chole same admission for gallstones)
⚠ no prophylactic antibiotics — only infected necrosis. Complications: necrosis, pseudocyst, ARDS, hypocalcemia

Bowel obstruction

SBO adhesions #1, hernias, tumor; colicky pain, bilious vomiting, distension, obstipation; dilated small bowel + air-fluid levels
SBO management NPO, IV fluids, electrolytes, NG decompression, serial exams; surgery if strangulation/closed loop/ischemia/peritonitis
LBO colorectal cancer #1, sigmoid volvulus (coffee-bean → endoscopic detorsion), stricture
Ogilvie colonic pseudo-obstruction (no mechanical cause) → supportive, decompression, neostigmine after excluding obstruction
Strangulation = operate
Red flags constant pain, fever, tachycardia, peritonism, ↑lactate, leukocytosis

Diverticulitis & mesenteric ischemia

Diverticulitis
Picture LLQ pain, fever, leukocytosis; CT diagnostic (Hinchey stage)
Manage bowel rest ± selective antibiotics; abscess → drain; perforation → surgery; avoid acute colonoscopy → scope at ~6 wk to exclude cancer
Mesenteric ischemia
Acute (small bowel, SMA) embolic (AF), thrombotic, NOMI (shock/pressors), venous (hypercoagulable); pain out of proportion, acidosis → CT angio, emergency
Chronic postprandial pain, food fear, weight loss ("intestinal angina")
Ischemic colitis elderly, watershed (splenic flexure), crampy pain then bloody diarrhea; usually supportive; surgery if gangrene

Anorectal · hernias · perioperative

Internal hemorrhoids painless BRBPR, grade I–IV; fiber/banding/surgery; bleeding is dx of exclusion — rule out CRC
Anal fissure severe pain with defecation, posterior midline; fiber, topical CCB/nitrate, sitz baths
Perianal abscess fluctuant, painful → incision & drainage
Hernia reducible → incarcerated (irreducible) → strangulated (ischemic, surgical emergency); femoral higher strangulation risk
Consent capacity + risks/benefits/alternatives incl. no treatment
Preop optimize anemia (IV iron), cardiac risk, anticoagulation; VTE + antibiotic prophylaxis

Colorectal cancer & pitfalls

CRC adenoma→carcinoma; right = anemia/occult bleed, left = obstruction/caliber change; colonoscopy + biopsy, staging CT, CEA → resection (hemicolectomy) ± adjuvant
Pain out of proportion mesenteric ischemia until proven otherwise
Don't scope acute diverticulitis (perforation)
IDA / BRBPR never blame hemorrhoids until CRC excluded
Free air / rigid abdomen → OR, not more imaging
Topic 10 / Acute Abdomen & GI Surgery (detailed) · generated from your 11-week guides · verify against course & clinical guidelines · review aid only

guides.title: Topic 11 GI Bleeding
guides.html:
◇ Topic Reference · 11 ◇

GI Bleeding

spans GI I + GI IV (upper & lower)
trigger → answer
Localize by what comes out — but resuscitate first
Hematemesis / coffee-ground = upper. Melena (black, tarry) = usually upper (blood digested). Hematochezia (bright/maroon) = usually lower — but a brisk upper bleed can present as hematochezia in an unstable patient.
BUN:creatinine ↑ favors upper (digested blood = protein load).
Always first: ABCs, two large-bore IVs, crystalloid, type & cross, monitor; the resuscitation precedes the diagnosis.
Risk scores: Glasgow-Blatchford (pre-endoscopy; identifies very-low-risk for outpatient care), Rockall (mortality/rebleed).
★ Massive / unstable bleed
Resuscitate to endoscopy: 2 large IVs, balanced crystalloid, restrictive transfusion (Hb threshold ~70, ~80 if cardiac), platelets if <50 + bleeding, FFP/PCC + reverse anticoagulation. Erythromycin/NG before scope to clear the stomach. Urgent endoscopy when stabilized.
Suspected variceal bleed (known cirrhosis/portal HTN): add octreotide + ceftriaxone + PPI → urgent band ligation; balloon tamponade or TIPS as rescue. Over-transfusion raises portal pressure → rebleed.

Resuscitation & assessment

Airway/Breathing/Circulation protect airway if hematemesis/altered; 2 large-bore IVs
Transfuse restrictive Hb ~70 (improves survival vs liberal); platelets <50 + bleeding; FFP/PCC for coagulopathy
Reverse hold/reverse anticoagulants & antiplatelets per bleeding severity
Stratify Glasgow-Blatchford (triage), Rockall (prognosis)
Prep scope IV erythromycin / NG lavage to improve visualization

Upper GI bleed

Causes peptic ulcer #1, esophagogastric varices, Mallory-Weiss, erosive gastritis/esophagitis, malignancy, Dieulafoy, AVM, aortoenteric fistula (prior graft)
Manage (non-variceal) high-dose IV PPI → endoscopy within 24 h; endoscopic hemostasis (clips/thermal/injection); Forrest class predicts rebleed
After H. pylori test & treat, stop NSAIDs
Variceal octreotide + ceftriaxone + band ligation (see emergencies)

Lower GI bleed

Causes diverticulosis #1 (painless), angiodysplasia (elderly; Heyde = AS + angiodysplasia), hemorrhoids, ischemic colitis, neoplasm/polyp, IBD, post-polypectomy, infectious colitis
Work-up (stable) colonoscopy after prep
Work-up (unstable/massive) CT angiography → embolization, or surgery; tagged RBC scan for slow bleeds
⚠ if unstable hematochezia, consider an upper source (do EGD) — brisk UGIB mimics LGIB

Variceal bleeding (focused)

Acute octreotide (splanchnic vasoconstriction) + ceftriaxone (improves survival) + PPI → band ligation
Rescue balloon tamponade (Sengstaken-Blakemore), TIPS
Prophylaxis non-selective β-blocker (propranolol/nadolol/carvedilol) and/or EVL — not for the acute bleed
Transfusion restrictive — over-transfusion ↑portal pressure → rebleed

Obscure / occult bleeding

Occult positive FOBT/FIT or iron-deficiency anemia with normal initial scopes
IDA in older adult colonoscopy + EGD to exclude malignancy
Obscure (negative scopes) small-bowel source → capsule endoscopy; angiodysplasia common

Pitfalls

Melena treat as upper until proven otherwise
Unstable hematochezia rule out a brisk upper source (EGD)
Restrictive transfusion Hb ~70 — liberal worsens outcomes (esp. varices)
Ceftriaxone in cirrhotic GI bleed improves survival — don't omit it
Aortoenteric fistula consider in anyone with a prior aortic graft + GI bleed
